coredata.dc:title: Simulation of Li-ion battery electrolyte vapour dispersion in an enclosed and quiescent environment: an experimental and computational fluid dynamics approach

coredata.dc:description: The leakage of electrolyte from a Li-ion battery cell can generate a dangerous atmosphere in enclosed environments due to the high volatility, flammability and toxicity of common electrolyte solvents like dimethyl carbonate (DMC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC). The use of early warning and detection systems is essential to detect the leakage, even after the solvents have fully evaporated, to limit human exposure. To aid in their design, this work introduces a novel computational fluid dynamics (CFD) dispersion-evaporation coupled model, which was validated by measuring the dispersion of electrolyte vapours in an enclosed 1 m3 box with no induced flow. The Large Eddy Simulation (LES-CFD) dispersion-evaporation model produced good results, as it demonstrated good agreement with experimental measurements at 15 and 30 cm above the source for both DMC and DEC. Experimental and simulated results also show a two-fold dispersion mechanism, a convection-based effect dependent on the density of the heavy vapour cloud gasses and surrounding environment, and a diffusion-based effect, which becomes dominant once the ambient density has homogenised across the domain.

head.ce:title: Simulation of Li-ion battery electrolyte vapour dispersion in an enclosed and quiescent environment: an experimental and computational fluid dynamics approach

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The leakage of electrolyte from a Li-ion battery cell can generate a dangerous atmosphere in enclosed environments due to the high volatility, flammability and toxicity of common electrolyte solvents like dimethyl carbonate (DMC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC). The use of early warning and detection systems is essential to detect the leakage, even after the solvents have fully evaporated, to limit human exposure. To aid in their design, this work introduces a novel computational fluid dynamics (CFD) dispersion-evaporation coupled model, which was validated by measuring the dispersion of electrolyte vapours in an enclosed 1 m3 box with no induced flow.

The Large Eddy Simulation (LES-CFD) dispersion-evaporation model produced good results, as it demonstrated good agreement with experimental measurements at 15 and 30 cm above the source for both DMC and DEC. Experimental and simulated results also show a two-fold dispersion mechanism, a convection-based effect dependent on the density of the heavy vapour cloud gasses and surrounding environment, and a diffusion-based effect, which becomes dominant once the ambient density has homogenised across the domain.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The green energy transition has become one of the most important objectives for many countries on a global scale [1]. With ever-increasing quantities of greenhouse gasses, transitioning to low-carbon energy sources, like wind and solar, is essential to combat the threat that the effects of climate change pose [2]. Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have become a key-enabling technology for the energy transition in the mobility and energy storage sector due to their high energy density, high cycle stability, and fast charging rates [3].

Through years of constant improvement, LIBs have been proven to be a safe and reliable technology. Nevertheless, there has been a global push to address the intrinsic safety hazards that rechargeable batteries may pose. Not only are they prone to battery failure, which could lead to the generation of fires [4], but the individual components of the batteries have been recognised to be an environmental and human health hazards [5].

One of the main components of a battery cell is the electrolyte that permits ion transfer within the cell, allowing a flow of current. In addition, the lithium salt used (usually lithium hexafluorophosphate), the electrolyte mixture is composed of common organic solvents, which are the preferred electrolyte solvents because of their aprotic nature, as they avoid interference with electrochemical reactions [6]. Unfortunately, these organic solvents are known to be highly volatile, flammable [7], and toxic [8] compounds. A pristine, large-format prismatic cell may hold up to 35 ml of electrolyte [9,10], such that the electrolyte leakage may lead to dangerous atmosphere after evaporation. Unfortunately, the leakage of the electrolyte solution from a battery cell is a common fault that leads to battery failure [11].

The high volatility of electrolyte solvents renders visual confirmation of leakage difficult, introducing the need for a monitoring system that can identify the vapours generated by the solvents. Since there are no readily available off-the-shelf specific and quantitative solutions for vapour detection [12], the design of fit-for-purpose monitoring systems is necessary. The first step for designing such a system is to have a deep understanding of the underlying dynamics of vapour generation and dispersion in a quiescent environment. Since the evaporation of the electrolyte solution is not strongly influenced by the presence of the solute, as demonstrated by Hess et al. [13], other studies of evaporation and dispersion of electrolytes focused exclusively on the evaporation of the pure solvent [14,15]. Hence, this work was also performed by using only common solvents used in electrolyte solutions.

Electrolyte vapours have a tendency to behave as heavy gasses due to their greater than air density. The release of heavy gasses is a widely studied topic within industrial safety, with many models of heavy gas dispersion currently available. While empirical [16] and integral box models [17] have shown good agreement with experimental data; they have mostly been tested for open field gas releases, while still being limited to simple obstacle geometry. Hence, the use of a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) approach lends to the possibility of using a more complex geometry for the tested domain in order to simulate the dispersion of gasses [18–20] while generating more accurate results. The use of CFD is also extended for the simulation of other incidental scenarios, like the Li-ion battery thermal runaway model [21].

The aim of this work is to design and validate a coupled evaporation and dispersion model through the CFD approach, thus expanding the scope of previous work [22]. The considered scenario is of an instantaneous electrolyte leakage from a battery pack, which results in the formation of a pool of electrolyte that evaporates depending on the environmental conditions, similar to our previous work [22]. To replicate the conditions of this scenario, a scaled down evaporation and dispersion experimental campaign was performed. Through experimental evaporation experiments of free electrolyte solvents, where the evolution of the vapour cloud is measured in a confined volume, the vapour dispersion methodology of the coupled model was validated, while assessing the effects of temperature on the dispersion rate of the solvent vapours released after the electrolyte leakage.

The experimental installation consists of a 1 m3 (1 m × 1 m × 1 m) aluminium box. The injection experiments were performed with dimethyl carbonate (DMC, ≥99 %, Sigma-Aldrich) and diethyl carbonate (DEC, ≥99 %, Sigma-Aldrich) as Li-ion battery electrolyte representative solvents, and isopropyl alcohol (IPA, ≥99.8 %, Supelco) as the benchmark compound. The introduction of the evaporating liquid was performed through a custom-made dish at the base of the box, which was designed to minimise any additional effects that the injection of the liquid might have on the generation of the vapour cloud due to added turbulence within the environment. More details on the geometry of the box and dish are shown in Fig. 1 . A PT100 thermocouple was partially submerged into the liquid to track temperature changes during the evaporation. All measurements were performed after the box was completely sealed. Note that the only additional turbulence introduced occurs during the process of injection, where air is initially pushed into the box for circa 6 s before the solvent.

The geometry of the injection dish is an annulus disk with an outer radius of 4.67 cm, and an inner radius of 0.91 cm. For each experiment, 11 ml of compound was used to ensure that the surface of the dish was fully covered with the liquid. A photo-ionisation detector (PID) MiniPID series 2 (IonScience, detection range between 0 and 4000 ppm, and a measurement error of ±1 mV) was used to measure the concentration of the compound of interest in air and its evolution in time as the vapour cloud was generated. The distance from the base of the box of the PID was varied along the Y-axis between 15 and 30 cm in two distinct experimental campaigns. Each experiment was repeated three times for each measurement point. After each experiment, the box was flushed with dry pressurised air through air inlets to purge the accumulated vapours of the evaporated compounds.

A temperature gradient of about 0.26 ± 0.02 °C from the base to the top of the box was recorded for each run with a pair of PT100 thermocouples positioned at 68 and 99 cm from the base of the box. Experiments were performed at room temperature between 22.6 and 23.1 °C, with relative humidity ranging between 45 and 70 %.

Simulations for this work were performed with Fluent from Ansys Workbench 2021R2, and the postprocessing was done with Python version 3.12.0. The CFD approach functions by solving the governing equations of fluid motion within a computational domain discretised into smaller volumes, defined as cells. Two models were tested in this work, the Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) and the Large Eddy Simulation (LES) models, which differ in the way eddies are simulated.
The RANS model resolves instantaneous changes in the flow by time averaging and decomposition the Navier-Stokes equations into time-independent average values and instantaneous fluctuations [23]:

The Shear Stress Transport (SST) model is a robust closure model that has shown reliable results when simulating the dispersion of rarefied gasses [24]. The SST k-ω turbulence model was used for all RANS simulations, while enabling the Low Reynolds correlations option to ensure simulation stability.

On the other hand, the LES model models the time-dependent Navier-Stokes equations by resolving most of the flow through its mesh and using its cells to simulate eddy generation and dissipation. Eddy simulation occurs until a certain size of the representative length of the eddy is reached, at which point the eddy is filtered and modelled instead of being simulated [23]:

The main difference from the RANS equation is the use of resolved velocities like

represent the Subgrid-Scale Stresses (SGS) that model the dissipation of eddies smaller than the nominal size of the cells within the computational domain. Filtering of the eddies is needed as, once the eddies reach the size of the cell, it is no longer representative of the phenomenon. The SGS closure model used for all simulations performed in this work is the Wall-Adaptive Local Eddy (WALE) model.

In the previous work [22], the LES model has demonstrated its ability to accurately reproduce the temperature gradient in the box, as described in the previous section, while the RANS model could not. In this work, both models were tested with experimental dispersion data to assess the reliability of the RANS model when compared to the LES model for the dispersion of vapours.

To accurately capture the phenomena of electrolyte-solvent vapour dispersion, evaporation models capable of modelling the evaporation process in a quiescent, enclosed environment are necessary. In previous work [22], two approaches gave acceptable results when compared to experimental data. As such, both were tested in this work:
•
The Kelly-Zion approach, based on the work by Kelly-Zion et al. [25] uses a composite Sherwood equation to account for multiple contributions to the evaporation process. This method was applied to the CFD model through a simple correlation in the evaporating source.
•
The Dollet approach, based on the work by Dollet et al. [26] uses a spatially-dependent model that divides the evaporating source into three zones that contribute to the overall evaporation rate at a given temperature. This method was applied to the CFD model by accounting for the coordinates of each individual cell in the evaporating source relative to the origin point frame of reference of the computational domain.

Both approaches were originally designed for a disk-shaped evaporating source, which is not representative of the geometry used in this work. Depending on the approach, equations used from Kelly-Zion et al. and Dollet et al.'s work were accordingly modified to account for effects that an annuli-shaped evaporating source may have. Furthermore, both models use bulk quantities to model the convective-based contribution within the evaporation equations. As such, bulk quantities were recorded from the CFD model at a chosen bulk distance in the computational domain to couple the evaporation model with the dispersion model. The chosen bulk distance for this work is the characteristic length of the injection dish of 4.67 cm, i.e., the outer radius of the injection dish.

To account for the changes from a disk-shaped to an annulus-shaped evaporating source, the expected contribution of the area within the inner circumference is subtracted from the overall evaporation rate. Thus, only the evaporated mass from the area between the outer and inner circumferences is introduced in the computational domain. All simulations performed with the Kelly-Zion approach used eq. (7) as the evaporation rate.

For the Dollet approach, a more complex implementation method was used. The original model by Dollet et al. suggested that the evaporating surface of a disk can be divided into three zones: an edge zone, an intermediate zone, and a centre zone.

By assuming that the descriptions made for the disk-shaped evaporating source are transferable to the annuli-shaped evaporating source, the inner and outer circumferences of the injection dish were considered as edges, with the intermediate and centre zones placed between them, as shown in Fig. 2 . Dollet et al. proposed that the centre zone can be excluded due to its small size compared to the intermediate zone, simplifying the evaporation rate model as follows:

To simulate the entirety of the evaporation process, a temperature model coupled with the evaporation model was used, so that temperature changes also affected the evaporation rate. While other works that have to deal with convective evaporation assume a constant temperature of the liquid phase [30–33], the use of a dynamically changing temperature model allows to account for changes within the computational domain, i.e., the surrounding environment of the source. This approach provides a more accurate representation of the inlet boundary condition. An important aspect of the approach presented in this work is the simulation of the evaporation of electrolyte solvents without having to simulate the liquid phase, instead just introducing mass through an inlet with a gaseous flowrate equivalent to the Eqs. (7) and (16), in Section 2.2.1. The concentration of the mass flow introduced was determined by assuming that the material boundary layer above the vapour-liquid interface dissipates following the mass-transfer theory of diffusion in a stagnant film [28]. The concentration at the edge of the material boundary layer was modelled by using the following equations:

This approach allowed for the modelling of the dissipation of the vapours until the edge of the material boundary layer, as shown in Fig. 4 . As such, the material boundary layer is not simulated, instead the vapour that diffuses from the layer to the surrounding environment is simply introduced in the computational domain. Eq. (24) was used to define the concentration for all evaporation flows simulated in this work.

Simulation of the vapour phase described in the previous section was done by using the compound properties listed in Table 1 . The liquid phase section was used to model the heat transfer with the surrounding environment from Section 2.2.2, while the vapour phase section was used for the evaporation model and dispersion approach of the generated vapours.

By means of diffusive-evaporation experiments, Seo et al. [15] demonstrated that there is a strong dependency on vapour pressure when estimating the binary diffusion coefficient of electrolyte solvents, like DMC. Seo et al. used a vapour pressure value of 2400 Pa to calculate a binary diffusion coefficient of 1.97 × 10−5 m2/s at 21 °C for DMC. Nevertheless, multiple sources report a higher vapour pressure of DMC at 22 °C through experimental data expressed as semi-empirical Antoine equations, resulting in 6110 Pa at 21 °C [47].

Choosing either the low vapour pressure value (2400 Pa at 21 °C) or the high vapour pressure value (6110 Pa at 21 °C) is important for the simulation approach, because it has a strong impact on the liquid-vapour interface modelling described in Section 2.2.3.

For this reason, both vapour pressure values were used for the simulation of evaporation and dispersion of DMC:
•
For low vapour pressure DMC simulations, the vapour pressure of 2400 Pa used by Seo et al. was used, along with their experimentally calculated binary diffusivity coefficient of 1.97 × 10−5 m2/s at 21 °C. These simulations are indicated as DMC with a low vapour pressure approach (DMC-LVP).
•
For high vapour pressure DMC simulations, the vapour pressure Antoine equation in Table 2 was used. Furthermore, since Seo et al.'s approach for the calculation of the binary diffusivity coefficient uses a low vapour pressure, it was instead calculated by using Marrero and Mason's semi-empirical correlation as reported in Table 2. The diffusion coefficient calculated with Marrero and Mason's equation was 9.42 × 10−6 m2/s at 21 °C. These simulations are indicated as DMC with a high vapour pressure approach (DMC-HVP).

Other important parameters for the analysis of the potential impact of the studied solvents are the Protection Action Criteria values (PAC), which are concentration thresholds for 1-h exposures [53] to assess the risks of the exposure to toxic compounds like battery electrolyte components. These are listed in Table 3 .
Consequences of exposure at the concentrations listed in Table 3 can vary from mild (PAC-1) to life-threatening (PAC-3).

The geometry and mesh of the computational domain for the simulations is shown in Fig. 5 . Small objects like cables and thermocouples were not modelled as they have little consequence on the dispersion of the vapours. This was mostly due to the small volume these objects occupy compared to the computational domain. While was also true for the PID sensors, they were nevertheless modelled to describe the sensing device more accurately.

As described in Section 2.1, the box is a 1 m3 volume cube. The height of the sensors H
PID was changed from between 15 cm to 30 cm to measure the concentrations of the compound of interest at different heights and compare them with simulated results.

Bulk values described in Section 2.2.1 were taken at a distance equivalent to the bulk distance of 4.67 cm and the height of the injection dish of 0.75 cm, for a total of 5.42 cm from the base of the box, above the injection dish.

A polyhedral mesh was used for all simulations in this work, as a mesh able to produce a high-quality concentration gradient was needed to accurately represent the dispersion of vapours with concentrations in the ppm range. The nominal cell size of the mesh was 5 mm, for a total of 3.4 million polyhedral cells for the entire domain. By applying the symmetry plane approach, the domain was halved, reducing the total number of cells needed to carry out each simulation to 1.7 million cells. To ensure that both vapour concentration and temperature gradients are accurately simulated near the evaporating source, an inflation layer was applied above the injection dish, as shown in Fig. 5.

The inflation layer has a total thickness of 1 cm and a growth rate of 1.1. It was divided into 18 layers, with the smallest layer being 0.4 mm thick. Mesh parameters presented in Table 4 have been validated in previous work with a similar geometry for both RANS and LES models [22]. Considerations for the mesh independence were included in the Supplementary Material.

The boundary conditions of each surface within the computational domain were mostly the same between the RANS and LES models. Wall conditions differed between the two approaches, because the roughness height parameter was used in the RANS simulations to model the effect of viscosity for wall-bounded flows [23], while it was not necessary for LES simulations. Since the walls of the box were made of aluminium, wall boundary conditions were given a roughness height value of 1 × 10−6 m [54] for the RANS model. A no-slip condition was also applied, and since experimental tests were done in an enclosed environment, no outlet was defined.

Species transport was enabled with multicomponent diffusion and inlet diffusion. The gaseous phases of the solvents were defined using the vapour properties and compound interactions listed in Tables 1 and 2. Dry air and water vapour were included to replicate the relative humidity values measured during the experiments. The incompressible ideal gas density model was used for both RANS and LES models. Evaporation, temperature and vapour-liquid interface models from Sections 2.2.1, 2.2.2 and 2.2.3 were included in the simulation through a custom-made user-defined function (UDF). A full explanation of how the UDF was setup can be found elsewhere [22]. Initialisation of each simulation was performed by patching an initial temperature of the evaporating source, a temperature profile for the box along the Y-axis, and a fixed humidity value, as indicated in Table 5 .

All simulations that utilised the RANS model had a pressure-velocity coupled solver and used a second upwind scheme to resolve all conservation equations. Simulations with the LES model used the bounded central differencing method for the momentum equation. Convergence criteria were kept as Fluent default values. In previous work [22], a timestep independence between 0.25, 0.5, 1 and 2 s timesteps was performed, showcasing little variation in dispersion results between simulations. To initialise the simulation, a flow of air was introduced in the domain to reduce computational instabilities for both evaporation approaches with a 0.05 s timestep, and to replicate the air flow experimentally introduced in the box during injection (see Section 2.1). After 5 simulated seconds, the timestep values were changed, and the solvent vapour was also introduced. To ensure that the convergence criteria are met, all simulations were performed with a 0.5 s timestep. Simulations were run for 2400 simulated seconds, with an average CPU time of 15 s per timestep.

Model performance was evaluated by using Hanna's approach [55], where the Geometric Variance (GV) and the Geometric Mean Bias (GM) were calculated from predicted and observed results. GV was used to quantify model-based variability of the predicted data compared to the observed data, while GM was used to define the tendency of over or underprediction of the model compared to the observed data:
(25)
GV = exp
ln

/

GM = exp
ln

Y p
¯
(27)
ln
GV
=
ln
GM
2
where
Y p
is the predicted data point by the model, while
Y o
is the observed data from experimental tests, which was estimated by accounting for the arithmetic average value of all experimental trials. By definition, the minimum GV for any given GM value was also plotted with Eq. (27), to indicate the lower limit of GV values between a 0.25 and 4 GM range. According to Chang et al. [56], a model is considered good when GV < 1.6, 0.7 < GM < 1.3. Results with GM < 1 indicate that the model overpredicts experimental data, while values GM > 1 indicate that the model underpredicts experimental data.

The Factor of Two (FAC2) was also used to indicate whether the results are within the acceptability range indicated in Eq. (28):
(28)
0.5 ≤ FAC 2 =
Y p

Lastly, the standard error from the mean (SEM) was used to define sample deviation from the mean, as shown in Eq. (29):
(29)
SEM = ±
SD

where SD is the standard deviation and
n s
is the total number of samples.

The arithmetic average of measured concentrations and simulated concentrations in time of IPA, DMC and DEC at 15 and 30 cm are presented in this section. Note that Fig. 6B and Fig. 6C show the same experimental data for DMC, but use different values for the vapour pressure of DMC, as mentioned in Section 2: Fig. 6B reports simulated results for DMC-LVP simulations, while Fig. 6C shows results for DMC-HVP simulations. All simulated results presented in this section were generated with the LES model. The SEM estimated from the sampled experimental trials had the following ranges:
•
Measurements of IPA had a minimum and maximum SEM of 2 and 34.8 ppm for 15 cm, 2.3 and 13.6 ppm for 30 cm, respectively.
•
Measurements of DMC had a minimum and maximum SEM of 1.1 and 164 ppm for 15 cm, and 0.1 and 39.7 ppm for 30 cm, respectively.
•
Measurements of DEC had a minimum and maximum SEM of 1.8 and 16.1 ppm for 15 cm, and 0.1 and 6.4 ppm for 30 cm, respectively.

Experimental results show that the concentration increase is not a monotonous function it time. Furthermore, depending on the distance at which measurements are taken, the dispersion rate varies significantly. For values measured at 15 cm:
•
IPA (see Figs. 6A and 7A) had an initial dispersion rate of 0.18 ppm/s at 360 s, which quickly peaked at 0.7 ppm/s at around 720 s. Afterwards, a steady decline of dispersion rate explains the plateauing of experimental data seen in Fig. 6A. The minimum and maximum SEM is 0.003 and 0.2 ppm/s, respectively.
•
DMC (see Fig. 6B/6C and 8 A/9 A) had a more restrained dispersion rate growth, going from 0.25 ppm/s at 300 s to its peak at 0.85 ppm/s at 1140 s. The dispersion rate of DMC then remained quasi-constant, with an average value of 0.71 ppm/s. The minimum and maximum SEM is 0.009 and 0.41 ppm/s, respectively.
•
DEC (see Figs. 6D and 10A) showed a significantly smaller dispersion rate growth than the other studied compounds, with an initial dispersion rate of 0.081 ppm/s at 720 s, and peaking at 0.128 ppm/s at 1080 s. Similar to DMC, it also shows a quasi-constant dispersion rate after peaking, with an average value of 0.1 ppm/s. The minimum and maximum SEM is 0.001 and 0.036 ppm/s, respectively.

It is evident that while IPA and DMC have similar dispersion rates, their behaviour quickly changes once their peak is reached. On the other hand, DMC and DEC, show analogous behaviour, due to their chemical similarities, although DEC has a noticeably delayed and smaller dispersion rate than DMC.

At 30 cm from the base, a similar qualitative behaviour to their 15 cm counterpart was measured:
•
IPA (see Figs. 6A and 7B) had a delayed initial dispersion rate when compared to what was measured at 15 cm, with 0.08 ppm/s after 540 s. The dispersion rate did not reach a peak; rather, it remained at a quasi-constant value, with an average of 0.16 ppm/s. The minimum and maximum SEM is 0.003 and 0.1 ppm/s, respectively.
•
DMC (see Fig. 6B/6C and 8B/9B ) had a third of the initial dispersion rate of 15 cm, with 0.04 ppm/s at 360 s. While no peak was reached, the dispersion rate growth slowed significantly at around 1500 s, with dispersion rates varying between 0.25 and 0.29 ppm/s afterwards. The minimum and maximum SEM is 0.004 and 0.06 ppm/s, respectively.
•
DEC (see Fig. 6D and 10B) had a slower start than its 15 cm counterpart, with an initial dispersion rate of 0.002 ppm/s at 840 s, which peaked at 1500 s with 0.056 ppm/s. Afterwards, a quasi-constant value was reached, with a dispersion rate average of 0.042 ppm/s. The minimum and maximum SEM is 0.0016 and 0.016 ppm/s, respectively.

Differences between the measured concentration at 15 and 30 cm from the base are not only in order of magnitude, but also in the behaviour of the vapours. A more thorough look of these differences is explored in Section 3.3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical evaluation of dispersion models coupled with the evaporation models
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, a throughout look at the goodness of each evaporation approach is gauged by comparing concentration in time and space of experimental and simulated results, according to statistical parameters expressed in Section 2.6.

FAC2 statistical results indicate that both approaches fall within the ranges of acceptability more consistently for shorter distances, i.e., closer to the source. Excluding results from DMC-LVP, the Kelly-Zion approach consistently produces poorer predictions than the Dollet one. The percentage of acceptable values from Eq. (28) (i.e., the ratio between the number of values that fall within FAC2 range and the number of total values) within the ranges stated in Section 2.6 are listed in Table 6 . Scatter plots of simulated and experimental results, and their tendency to fall within the FAC2 range is shown in Fig. 11 . When values are above the central line, it indicates that experimental data is underpredicted, while when it is below it, it indicates an overprediction.

For DMC-HVP and DEC, the Kelly-Zion approach at 30 cm heavily underpredicted the measured data. And although it performs better at 15 cm, the Kelly-Zion approach remained unreliable for IPA and DMC-HVP, where less than 60 % of the considered values are in the range of acceptability for IPA, and none for DMC-HVP. On the other hand, there is a decisive improvement when using the DMC-LVP and DEC at 15 cm, but these values compare poorly to the Dollet approach, as it has good results for all compounds at both considered heights.

These results were confirmed when assessing the GM and GV values estimated and plotted in Fig. 12 . Most cases in which the Kelly-Zion approach was used resulted in the underprediction of the measured values, outside of the range of acceptability, with a single exception at 30 cm for DMC-LVP.

Overall, it was shown that the Kelly-Zion and Dollet approaches are capable of replicating the qualitative behaviour of most simulated compounds, with very little scatter for all cases, except for IPA at 30 cm (see Figs. 11A and 12). Nevertheless, the Dollet approach has consistently produced results with better agreement with experimental data from all compounds, thus demonstrating to be the better approach. Results from this work have remained consistent with previous work [22] where both Kelly-Zion and Dollet approaches were used, in which the Dollet approach generated more accurate results for the prediction of the evaporation flux of IPA, DMC and DEC.

As such, by only looking at the Dollet approach, and barring results measured at 15 cm for DMC-LVP, and 30 cm for IPA and DEC, all simulations that used this approach produced dispersion results that fall inside the range of “good” models as defined in Section 2.6. It is necessary to highlight that although being the better approach of the two studied in this work, the Dollet approach still has difficulty in simulating low concentration areas. This is shown in Fig. 6D, where a good qualitative, but poor quantitative, agreement between the experimental and simulated data can be seen for DEC at 30 cm. Possible underlying reasons for this behaviour are further expanded on Section 3.5.

The same cannot be said for IPA at 30 cm, as its dispersion dynamics is not reproduced qualitatively. A possible explanation for the significant discrepancy for IPA at 30 cm might be related to its underpredicted evaporation flux when using the Dollet approach, as reported in our previous work [22]. The inlet concentration defined by Eq. (24) increases with a lower evaporation rate; as such, an underprediction of the evaporation rate can lead to an overprediction of the inlet concentration of the vapour cloud. The significance of these values to the dispersion behaviour of the cloud is further explored in the following section.

As mentioned in Section 2.3 and previously shown in Fig. 6, the vapour pressure approach used had a significant effect on the dispersion speed for DMC. Thus, to assess the viability of the low vapour pressure approach (see Fig. 6B) and the high vapour pressure approach (see Fig. 6C) for DMC simulations, a thorough look at where these two approaches excel at simulating the dispersion process is necessary. The following analysis was done with the Dollet approach, due to it being the approach with the better fit: between the two, the DMC-HVP simulation produces better results at both measured distances when compared to the DMC-LVP simulation, as shown by results from Fig. 11. By then assessing the GM and GV values of the entire duration of the dispersion (see Fig. 12), the overpredicted dispersion rate by the DMC-HVP simulation produces better results than the DMC-LVP simulation. This is particularly significant as both simulated distances for DMC-HVP fall within the ranges of acceptability, while DMC-LVP at 15 cm does not. With this, it can be concluded that using the higher vapour pressure value and the lower binary diffusion coefficient calculated with Marrero and Mason's equation (see Section 2.3) can be considered a better methodology when compared to using Seo et al.'s values.

One of the main aspects of the CFD approach is the predictive capabilities of the dispersion model across the entire domain. Thus, the behaviour of the vapours generated during the evaporation process is represented in the domain by isosurfaces at a given concentration, as shown in Figs. 13 and 14
. Concentrations at PAC levels 2 and 3 were chosen for DMC and DEC, to show how fast the vapours at a critical concentration disperse in the 1 m3 domain. Concentrations for IPA at 200 and 1000 ppm were chosen to demonstrate its comparable behaviour to the electrolyte solvents during evaporation (see Fig. 14A). The analysis of the dispersion model was performed by only using the Dollet approach, as it generated the most accurate results among the proposed approaches.

The behaviour of heavy gasses was experimentally studied by Kelly-Zion et al. [57], where it was shown that vapours of sessile droplets of heavier-than-air compounds tend to accumulate around the evaporating source as a negative buoyancy jet [58,59]. Gasses simulated by Zhang et al. [60] also manifested the same negative flow, where the inertial force with which a denser gas than the surrounding environment is counteracted by buoyant forces, reversing the momentum of the gasses to move opposite to the up-flow.

It is then not surprising that all simulated vapours in this work disperse radially, with very little vertical dispersion not related to the initial inertial flow as the stream enters the domain. This is highlighted in Figs. 7 to 10, where the growing dispersion rate quickly relaxes into a plateau (except for IPA). A possible explanation for the observed trends is that they are a consequence of a two-fold contribution of convection and diffusion-based dispersion. Since electrolyte solvent vapours are heavier-than-air, they tend to accumulate at the bottom of the volume as they evaporate, where material natural convection aids radial dispersion of the vapours. The closer to the source, the stronger the convection-based dispersion will be. As density homogenisation in the volume increases, and the difference in density between the vapour cloud and the surrounding environment decreases, the convection-based contribution, and the main dispersion mechanism becomes diffusion-based, leading to a quasi-stable dispersion rate.

To simulate the vapour dispersion of these compounds, some essential parameters must be accurately chosen. Namely, the evaporation flux and inlet concentration significantly affect the speed of convection-based dispersion, while the binary diffusion coefficient between the tested compound and the most represented compound in the domain (i.e., air) influences the speed of diffusion-based dispersion. It is necessary to highlight that these dispersion contributions are not easily decoupled, but the effects of each parameter can be studied with results from this work. Furthermore, although no experimental measurements of the evaporation flux were performed in this work, the simulated evaporation flux still plays a crucial role in the behaviour of the vapour cloud, and as such, were used for this analysis of the dispersion model. More information on the validity of the Dollet approach as an evaporation model can be found in our previous work [22].

Consequences of a low evaporation flux and inlet concentration are also seen with the DEC simulation, which, although it has a similar binary diffusion coefficient to that of DMC-HVP, has a significantly lower dispersion rate (see Figs. 13B and 14B). The PAC-2 level cloud moves as fast as the isosurfaces at PAC-3 of DMC simulations, with the higher concentration isosurface remaining almost stationary for 20 simulated minutes. DEC is the heaviest compound (with a molar mass that is 31 % larger than DMC; see Table 2), which might have a positive effect on the convection-based dispersion mechanism as a higher molar mass leads to higher density with a lower compound concentration. Nevertheless, due to the highly limiting inlet concentration and evaporation flux, convection-based dispersion mechanisms is strongly dampened when compared to all other simulated compounds. Note that compared to simulated results, experimental data indicates a slower dispersion, which might signify that there might be other parameters that could slow the dispersion even further (see Table 8).

A more complicated relationship can be seen with the dispersed vapours of IPA and DMC-LVP (see Figs. 13A and 14A), whose vapour clouds evolve with similar dispersion rates, as shown in Figs. 7 and 8. Both compounds have similar dispersion speeds at the beginning of the simulation, but slow down at different rates, leading to higher concentrations at 15 cm simulated for IPA by 2400 simulated seconds. This behaviour also occurs at 30 cm, but much slower, as all phenomena are delayed farther away from the source, as mentioned in the previous section. Table 7 shows how IPA has an evaporation flux that is 31 % less than DMC-LVP, but has an inlet concentration that is twice as high. As IPA also has a binary diffusion coefficient that it half of that of DMC-LPV, and a molar mass (see Table 2) that is 66 % that of DMC, the fact that their vapour cloud evolves in a similar way, where IPA even supersedes its dispersion rate, speaks of the importance of the inlet concentration when compared to all other parameters.

Results from this work indicate that the dispersion rate for evaporated solvents is mostly influenced by the evaporation flux and inlet concentration used for the source term, along with the binary diffusion coefficient used to simulate the multicomponent diffusion within the domain. It is then reasonable to conclude that the choice of an appropriate model for the estimation of the binary diffusion coefficient is essential, as well as demonstrating that the liquid-vapour interface model introduced in Section 2.2.3 plays a pivotal role in simulating the complicated dispersion mechanism of the tested evaporated compounds. Videos of the first 1200 s of the dispersion of DMCH and DEC can be found within the Supplementary Material.

Results from this work indicate that the dispersion rate for evaporated solvents is mostly influenced by the evaporation flux and inlet concentration used for the source term, along with the binary diffusion coefficient used to simulate the multicomponent diffusion within the domain. It is then reasonable to conclude that the choice of an appropriate model for the estimation of the binary diffusion coefficient is essential, as well as demonstrating that the liquid-vapour interface model introduced in Section 2.2.3 plays a pivotal role in simulating the complicated dispersion mechanism of the tested evaporated compounds. Videos of the first 1200 s of the dispersion of DMCH and DEC can be found within the Supplementary Material.

While the temperature of the liquid phase does not have a direct effect on the dispersion, as the evaporated compound quickly mixes with air, and thus reaches similar temperatures to its surrounding environment, it is still a consequential parameter for the estimation of the evaporation rate model, as well as the modelling of the liquid-vapour interface that acts as an inlet boundary condition. Experimental and simulated results performed with the LES model are plotted in Fig. 15 .

In the previous work [22], the evaporating vessel was a Petri dish with an area that was 16 % larger than the injection dish used in this work. The evaporation of solvents presented here occurred at around 3 °C higher for all compounds than those performed in the Petri dish. Nevertheless, temperature changes in this work are lower than those in the previous work. This might be related to not only the materials the evaporation vessels were made out of, but also to their geometry. The Petri dish was made of borosilicate glass, which has a
λ borosilicate
= 1.15 W m−1/K [61], while the injection dish in this work is made of aluminium, with a
λ alluminium
= 237 W m−1/K [62]. As the liquid was surrounded by the aluminium vessel in this work, heat was more easily compensated during evaporation, with small drops of 0.34 °C, 0.33 °C and 0.07 °C for IPA, DMC and DEC, respectively.

The Kelly-Zion and Dollet approaches were performed in a similar manner for all simulated cases. For both approaches, the first significant temperature drop, which can be seen in Fig. 15A for IPA, and Fig. 15B for DMC, in the first 120 s, was replicated with varying accuracy. For IPA and DEC, both approaches had similar temperature drops, then remained stable for the duration of the simulated time. Interestingly, for DEC (see Fig. 15C), both approaches experienced the same slight temperature increase that began at around 240 s after the beginning of the simulation. The reason is related to the lowering of the evaporation rate of the simulated solvent, which signifies less heat removal, and thus a slight increase in temperature as an equilibrium is reached with the environment. For DMC, only the Kelly-Zion approach for the simulation of DMC-HVP had a significantly lower prediction of temperature decrease. This is mostly related to the tendency of the Kelly-Zion approach to predict evaporation rates that are significantly higher than for the Dollet approach, which was explored in previous work [22].
Due to the low temperature change, FAC2 = 1 for each tested approach, as they all fall within the range of acceptability. Additionally, Fig. 16 shows the GM and GV values for each approach.

Fig. 16 shows how, independent of the approach used, they all fall within the ranges of acceptability as defined in Section 2.6. GV values for both approaches in all simulations have the minimum value for a given GM, as no additional variance was found. While GM values vary slightly between approaches, these results show that almost every method produces good results for the modelling of the thermal balance of the evaporating system, as temperature changes are all within the same magnitude as experimental results, with only the DMC-HVP with the Kelly-Zion approach dropping beyond a 1 °C.

As was shown in previous sections, while the presented model manages to reproduce the general behaviour of the vapours generated during the evaporation of electrolyte solvents, it is still worth exploring possible reasons for certain discrepancies, like the underprediction of the concentration measured at 30 cm for DEC vapours while using the Dollet approach.

In the previous work, the choice to use the LES model rather than the industry standard RANS came as a consequence of the RANS model being unable to reproduce a consistent temperature gradient during the evaporation simulation. Furthermore, with higher simulation stability and little change in computational time to perform the same simulation, the LES model has been chosen. Since the focus of this work was dispersion and a different domain was simulated, a pair of IPA simulations with the Dollet approach were performed with the LES and RANS models to assess the difference between the two models and their validity when compared to experimental data. Results are shown in Fig. 17 . Simulations for both cases were initialised at 22.84 °C, 22.88 °C and 22.97 °C at 15, 30 and 60 cm height above the base, respectively. Temperature in the LES model accurately simulated the small gradient of 0.31 °C, while temperatures in the RANS model were homogenised at various heights to an average of 22.94 °C only a few simulated seconds later (see Fig. 17).

For this reason, the viability of the RANS model and the effect that a poorly defined temperature gradient may have on the dispersion of the vapours are studied in this work, where RANS simulations with each compound were performed and compared with experimental data. All compounds were simulated using the Dollet approach, as it was demonstrated in Section 3.1 to be the approach that yields the most reliable results. Solver setup for RANS simulations is described in Section 2.5.

Values of GM and GV between the LES results presented in Section 3.1 and the RANS model using the Dollet approach were plotted in Fig. 18 . Similar to the LES model, the RANS model mostly presents difficulties in simulating the dispersion for regions of low concentration.

When using the RANS model, changes were marginal for concentrations of DMC and DEC measured at 15 cm height from the base, while somewhat more significant for IPA. This remained the same for most cases measured at 30 cm, except for an improvement for DEC with the GM value, and for IPA for the GV value. Variations in predicted results of IPA at 15 and 30 cm were related to an increase in concentration at the beginning of the simulation while using the RANS model, which was not observed when using the LES model (see Fig. 19 ). This behaviour of the RANS model often led to computational instabilities, which were rectified as the simulations progressed, and the flow profile was defined within the box, leading the vapour cloud to evolve in the same manner as the LES model in the latter half of the simulation. DEC improvements at 30 cm in the RANS model are also related to the slight increase of concentration at the beginning of the simulation, but it might also be a consequence to a marginally faster dispersion rate compared to the LES model, with an average of 0.0202 ppm/s for the LES model, an average of 0.02022 ppm/s for the RANS model, which results in a smaller underprediction by the latter. The slight increase in dispersion rate for DEC may have been caused by the homogenisation of the temperature of the box of around 22.94 °C, which is slightly higher than the initialisation temperature of 22.83 °C at the 15 cm height. As such, the homogenised temperature and computational instabilities brought by the use of the RANS model did have a noticeable effect on the dispersion rate of simulated cases, specifically at low concentrations. Nevertheless, these effects were only marginal, as the overall behaviour of the cloud did not change, with similar results between the LES and RANS models for most compounds, as seen in Figs. 18 and 19.

Zhang et al.'s work also focused on the dispersion of a sulphur hexafluoride, an inert heavy gas, while compering results from different RANS models and results from dispersion experiments in an environment with no induced ventilation [60]. Even with their best performing model, the RANS SST k-ω, results from low concentration areas tended to fall outside of the ranges of acceptability while still having good qualitative agreement, similar to what is seen at 30 cm for DEC simulations in this work. It is then safe to assume that low concentration areas tend to be difficult to simulate, regardless of the turbulent model used. Note that although minimal, the slight increase of temperature brought on by using the RANS simulation in this work did lead to an increase in its dispersion rate. Temperature drop predicted by both evaporation approaches in Fig. 15C could have an effect on the underprediction of DEC vapours at 30 cm, as the vapour introduced in the computational domain has a lower temperature than what was experimentally reported, lowering the dispersion rate. Better control of experimental parameters might allow for a better representation of temperature changes found within the box, leading to better predictions. It is important to highlight that although the quantitative prediction is not ideal, the qualitative behaviour of DEC is very well represented, demonstrating that using only initial conditions of the evaporation process is able to generate a reliable representation of the dispersion of electrolyte vapours.

For this work, the use of the RANS model could have been a viable option to simulate the dispersion of vapours in a quiescent environment. Nevertheless, for a system that is more sensitive to temperature changes, like DEC, where an accurate depiction of the temperature gradient in the computational domain is necessary, the use of the LES model would be the most appropriate choice.

Experiments to track the evolution of a vapour cloud generated by the evaporation of Li-ion battery electrolyte solvents, DMC and DEC, were carried out. The results were used to create an accurate dispersion model that could aid the design of early warning systems in enclosed or poorly ventilated environments where accumulation of toxic and flammable vapours can be a risk. To this end, this work introduces a novel model that couples evaporation with dispersion while modelling the liquid-vapour interface of an evaporating solvent through the mass-transfer theory of diffusion in a stagnant film. Thus, simulations with an LES-CFD evaporation-dispersion coupled model with the proposed Kelly-Zion and Dollet evaporation approaches were carried out.

Experimental concentration measurements were performed with PID sensors at 15 and 30 cm from the base of the box at room temperatures between 22.6 and 23.1 °C. PAC-2 level concentration (concentration thresholds for 1-h exposures where irreversible effects start manifesting once exposed) at 15 cm were reached in an average time of 8 min for DMC and 29 min for DEC. At 30 cm, PAC-2 levels were reached in an average time of 24 min for DMC and were not reached within the analysed time for this work for DEC. The behaviour of the vapours was successfully reproduced through the CFD model introduced in this work, with the Dollet approach consistently outperforming the Kelly-Zion approach by predicting concentration values at the given distances with higher accuracy. Thus, the presented work demonstrated that good results may be obtained through the following:
•
The use of the LES model as a turbulence, which allows for a more accurate representation of the temperature gradients within the simulated domain when compared to industry standard models, like the RANS model.
•
The coupling the evaporation and local heat balance model that is capable of linking liquid temperature change to evaporation rate and dispersion speed.
•
The introduction of a novel liquid-vapour interface model that estimates the vapour dissipation within the material boundary layer above the liquid surface.

It was demonstrated that while the RANS turbulence model may produce good results, for systems that are sensitive to temperature changes, on the liquid or within the computational domain, better results are obtained when using the LES model and the Dollet approach.

The proposed model has shown its capabilities in predicting the evaporation and dispersion of electrolyte solvents. Application for this model extends beyond the case scenario of leakage from a battery cell stored in a car, as it could be relevant for leakage scenarios during storage and transportation, as well as during recycling, where enclosed environments with little ventilation might lead to the formation of toxic atmospheres. Future work involves the application of the CFD evaporation-dispersion approach to a larger computational domain that could help define the dispersion of Li-ion battery electrolyte solvent vapours after an accidental leakage. Additionally, the methodology presented in this work is fully transferable to scenarios with forced convection evaporation, once a suitable and validated evaporation model is provided. The presented model can be a starting point to build a more generalised one, for both natural and forced convection flow conditions. Further work should focus on the adaption of the evaporation-temperature coupled approach with appropriate forced convection equations, along with updating the liquid-vapour interface model with an appropriate estimation of the material boundary layer thickness specifically catered for forced convection environments.
The use of this approach will help generate sufficient data for engineering parameters necessary to aid the design of early warning systems and indoor ventilation.
